1: The sons of Judah open the extended tribal genealogy — Perez and Hezron appear in the Matthean genealogy of Christ (Matthew 1:3). As the tribe from which the Messiah descends (Genesis 49:10; Micah 5:2; Revelation 5:5), every name in this Judahite genealogy is a strand in the line that leads to Jesus. The Chronicler’s careful preservation of these names represents the covenant community’s guardianship of the messianic lineage across generations of exile.
2: Clans of the Zorathites — Zorah was the city of Samson (Judges 13:2), another judge-deliverer whose life prefigures Christ: set apart from birth, empowered by the Spirit, engaged in single-handed combat against the enemies of God’s people. The continuation of this tribal memory in the genealogy keeps the deliverer-lineage visible even in a list of names.
3: Founders of settlements named within Judah’s territory — the theme of founding families establishing places in the promised land runs throughout this genealogy. As a theme: the covenant community’s rootedness in the land prefigures the eschatological inheritance secured by Christ (Hebrews 11:16; Revelation 21:2), a city with foundations whose architect and builder is God.
4: Penuel and Ezer, founders of Gedor and Hushah — sons of Hur, the firstborn of Ephrathah, and father of Bethlehem. The mention of Bethlehem’s founding lineage carries particular Christological weight: Bethlehem is not only David’s city but the birthplace prophesied for the Messiah (Micah 5:2; Matthew 2:6). The city’s founding ancestors are traceable in this genealogical record.
5: Ashhur the founder of Tekoa — Tekoa was the home of the wise woman who advised David (2 Samuel 14:2) and of the prophet Amos (Amos 1:1). This genealogy preserves the civic founders of the Judahite towns that were part of the covenant community’s ongoing life. As a revelation of God: YHWH works through specific families in specific places, the antithesis of an abstract deity unconnected to particular human histories.
6: Naarah’s sons — the listing of children from both wives of Ashhur shows the Chronicler tracking the full scope of Judah’s multiplication in the land. As a theme: the fruitfulness of the covenant community (fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise in Genesis 12:2) runs through these genealogical records, each name evidence of YHWH keeping his promise of blessing.
7: Sons of Helah — more Judahite descendants. The sheer density of names witnesses to the covenant community’s survival through bondage, wilderness, conquest, and the divided monarchy. Each name represents a life in which YHWH’s covenant faithfulness was operative, even unrecorded. As a theme: the preservation of the covenant people across generations is YHWH’s sustained fulfillment of the promise to Abraham (Genesis 15:5).
8: Koz’s line — clans of the Judahite territory. As a revelation of God: the Chronicler’s meticulous genealogical record-keeping reflects YHWH’s own knowing of each member of the covenant community by name (Isaiah 43:1; John 10:3: the Good Shepherd “calls his own sheep by name”). The genealogy is a covenant register, not merely a tribal inventory.
9: Jabez — embedded in the genealogy as a singular figure: “more honoured than his brothers.” His name means pain (ʿōṣeḇ) — he was born under a shadow. The grace theme of this verse is striking: a name implying curse becomes the occasion for conspicuous divine blessing. The pattern anticipates Christ, who was “despised and rejected” (Isaiah 53:3) yet is now the most honored of all (Philippians 2:9-11).
10: Jabez’s prayer is the theological centrepiece of the chapter. “Oh, that you would bless me and enlarge my territory!” — the prayer breaks the curse implied in his name through simple direct address to “the God of Israel.” God grants the request. The movement from name-of-pain to blessing-through-prayer types the gospel movement: from curse (Galatians 3:10) to blessing through the one who became a curse for us (Galatians 3:13). The enlargement of territory anticipates the nations as Christ’s inheritance (Psalms 2:8).
11: Chelub, Shuah’s brother — more Judahite clan founders. The genealogical chain continues. As a theme: every generation of the covenant community existed within the unbroken chain of promise that would ultimately produce the Messiah, even when the individuals had no awareness of their place in that lineage. The genealogy makes visible the hidden scaffolding of redemptive history.
12: Tehinnah the founder of Ir-nahash — “men of Recah.” The founding of cities and settlement of the land are covenant acts: YHWH gave the land to Abraham’s descendants (Genesis 15:18-21), and each new settlement is a concrete fulfillment of that promise. As a theme: the incarnation of Christ in a specific place (Bethlehem of Judah) is the ultimate fulfillment of this land-promise trajectory.
13: Sons of Kenaz: Othniel and Seraiah. Othniel was the first judge of Israel (Judges 3:9-11) — the Spirit-empowered deliverer who rescued Israel. As a type: Othniel’s rescue through the Spirit’s empowerment prefigures the pattern of Spirit-anointed deliverance reaching its fulfillment in Christ (Luke 4:18; Acts 10:38).
14: Joab the founder of Ge-harashim (valley of craftsmen) — “for they were craftsmen.” The Spirit-filled artisans of the tabernacle (Bezalel, Exodus 31:1-11) and Jesus the carpenter (tekton, Mark 6:3) are both evoked by this lineage. God’s Son entered human life as a craftsman before his public ministry — the covenant God is not disengaged from manual labor.
15: Sons of Caleb son of Jephunneh — Caleb, the faithful spy who followed YHWH wholeheartedly (Numbers 14:24; Joshua 14:8-9), is a significant type of faith securing inheritance. The promise to Caleb — “the land on which your feet have walked will be your inheritance forever, because you wholly followed the LORD” (Joshua 14:9) — anticipates the inheritance promised to all who follow Christ (Colossians 3:24).
16: Sons of Jehallelel — names in Judah’s southern territory clans. As a revelation of God: YHWH does not merely make a general promise to a tribe but sustains specific families through specific generations, the personal and particular nature of covenant faithfulness that the NT presents as the ground of individual assurance (Romans 8:38-39).
17: Sons of Ezrah including Mered — and Mered married Bithiah, daughter of Pharaoh (v18). A Gentile woman of Egyptian royal lineage is incorporated into the Judahite tribe. As a type: the inclusion of Pharaoh’s daughter in Judah’s covenant genealogy anticipates the eschatological gathering of nations, fulfilled in Christ who breaks down the dividing wall (Ephesians 2:14-16).
18: Bithiah daughter of Pharaoh — a Gentile woman in Judah’s messianic genealogical record. The Chronicler’s inclusion parallels the Matthean genealogy’s inclusion of Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, and Bathsheba: Gentile women and women of unusual status are present in the line that leads to Christ, signaling that the Messiah would be “a light for revelation to the Gentiles” (Luke 2:32).
19: Sons of Hodiah’s wife — blended family lines included in the covenant record. The comprehensive inclusion of diverse family lines reveals YHWH as the God of every family and clan, anticipating Paul’s declaration that in Christ “there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female” (Galatians 3:28) — the messianic community transcends the social divisions that genealogies typically reinforce.
20: Sons of Shimon and Ishi — more Judahite clans. The name Ishi means “my husband” (ʾîšî), the same word used in Hosea 2:16 for the eschatological covenant marriage: YHWH declares “You will call me ‘my husband.’” The coincidence of name carries a thematic echo: the covenant community is always, in OT prophetic vision, the bride of YHWH — fulfilled in the church as the bride of Christ (Revelation 21:2, 9).
21: Sons of Shelah son of Judah — linen-workers at Beth-ashbea. Fine linen clothed the priests and the tabernacle; in Revelation 19:8, fine linen represents “the righteous acts of the saints” worn by the bride of Christ. The covenant community’s weavers literally clothed the sanctuary — a shadow of the eschatological community clothed in righteousness.
22: “These are ancient records.” The Chronicler acknowledges the antiquity of his source material. As a revelation of God: the care with which the covenant community preserved genealogical records across centuries testifies to YHWH’s interest in historical specificity. The NT similarly grounds the gospel in historical genealogy (Matthew 1:1-17; Luke 3:23-38) against any mythologizing tendency.
23: Potters in the king’s service. Paul uses the potter imagery specifically in the context of YHWH’s sovereign purposes in redemptive history (Romans 9:20-21; Jeremiah 18:6) — and the NT image of believers as clay in the Potter’s hands finds its literal OT grounding in exactly the kind of covenant-community craft work this verse describes.
24: Sons of Simeon — the Simeonite genealogy begins. Simeon’s territory lay within Judah’s allotment (Joshua 19:1-9). As a shadow: the gradual absorption of Simeon into Judah mirrors the way the covenant community is drawn into the messianic tribe — not by Simeon ceasing to exist but by the messianic tribe expanding to encompass it. The church similarly gathers all peoples into the one covenant community.
25: Simeonite genealogical line from Shaul to Shallum to Mibsam to Mishma. The preservation of the Simeonite clan record in the post-exilic period, when Simeon had long since been absorbed into Judah, reflects YHWH’s faithfulness to each covenant identity. As a revelation of God: no part of the covenant community is forgotten — each has a preserved record in YHWH’s book (Malachi 3:16; Revelation 21:12).
26: Sons of Mishma continuing the Simeonite line. The persistence of this genealogical record across exile reflects the post-exilic community’s determination to maintain covenant identity despite the Babylonian disruption — the same persistence that characterizes the NT church under persecution (Rev 2-3 letters).
27: “Their whole clan did not increase as much as the sons of Judah.” Judah’s greater multiplication corresponds to the Davidic-messianic promise that concentrated covenant blessing in Judah’s line. Jacob’s blessing (Genesis 49:8-12) set this trajectory; the demographic reality of the tribal period confirms it.
28: Simeonite settlements at Beer-sheba, Moladah, Hazar-shual — the southernmost fringe of the promised land. Beer-sheba was the site of Abraham’s covenant with Abimelech (Genesis 21:31) and the symbolic southern boundary of Israel. The Simeonites settling at this covenant-saturated site connects their habitation to the Abrahamic promise that finds its ultimate fulfillment in the new creation (Revelation 21:1-3).
29: Additional Simeonite settlements — Bilhah, Ezem, Tolad. The granular specificity of settlement records reflects YHWH’s superintendence of real geography: the covenant people occupy actual, named places. The same particularity characterizes the NT: Jesus was crucified outside Jerusalem (John 19:20), rose in a specific garden tomb (John 20:11), ascended from the Mount of Olives (Acts 1:12).
30: Bethuel, Hormah, Ziklag — Simeonite cities. Ziklag was David’s base during his time with the Philistines (1 Samuel 27:6) and the site of the Amalekite raid and rescue (1 Sam 30). The Chronicler’s note that these were “their cities until the time of David” (v31) connects Simeonite geography to the Davidic-messianic framing of the entire genealogical project.
31: “These were their cities until the time of David.” The Davidic reign is the theological orienting reference point. Throughout Chronicles, David is the centre toward whom the genealogies point and from whom the covenant trajectory proceeds. The “time of David” is the OT pivot — the establishment of the Davidic covenant that carries the messianic promise forward to Christ.
32: Five Simeonite villages including Etam and Rimmon. The careful enumeration of even small villages reflects YHWH’s concern with the particular: no settlement of his people is beneath divine attention. Jesus makes the same point with sparrows and numbered hairs (Matthew 10:29-30).
33: “They maintained their genealogical records.” The reflexive preservation of covenant lineage was itself a spiritual act — an assertion that YHWH’s promises were real and the covenant community was not dissolved by history. The NT image of names written in the Lamb’s book of life (Revelation 3:5; 1 Chronicles 21:27) is the eschatological counterpart of this earthly genealogical register.
34: Named leaders of Simeonite clans — Meshobab, Jamlech, Joshah. Each named leader represents a person responsible for a covenant community of families. As a revelation of God: YHWH works through named, particular human leaders to shepherd his people — the same pattern that runs through judges, kings, and apostles to its completion in Christ as the one named Leader who holds all authority (Matthew 28:18).
35: Joel and Jehu — Simeonite clan leaders. The name Joel (yôʾēl = YHWH is God) persistently embedded in Israelite naming practices signals the theological convictions carried in names. The cumulative effect of theophoric names throughout the genealogies is a sustained implicit confession of covenant identity across generations.
36: Elioenai and other Simeonite leaders. The name Elioenai (ʾelîʿênay = toward YHWH are my eyes) is a prayer embedded in a name: perpetual orientation toward YHWH. This posture of the whole covenant community — sustained orientation toward YHWH — is what Hebrews 12:2 describes as “fixing our eyes on Jesus, the pioneer and perfecter of faith.”
37: Ziza son of Shiphi — the genealogical chain reaching back through multiple generations. As a theme: Christ’s own genealogy is traced with precision (Matthew 1:1-17) precisely to establish his specific covenantal location within the line of Abraham and David. The deep genealogical memory of the covenant community is what makes such precise tracing possible.
38: “Their families grew greatly in number.” The multiplication of the covenant community fulfills the Abrahamic blessing of fruitfulness. The NT counterpart: the rapid growth of the early church (Acts 2:41; 1 Chronicles 4:4; 1 Chronicles 6:7) is interpreted as divine multiplication — the same covenant-blessing pattern operating in the new covenant community gathered around Christ.
39: Simeonites journeying to find pasture for their flocks — a pastoral migration. The shepherd-flock imagery underlying this narrative evokes the theological metaphor of YHWH as shepherd leading his people to good pasture (Psalms 23:2; John 10:9: “he will come in and go out and find pasture”).
40: Finding good pasture — “rich, good pasture in a broad, peaceful, undisturbed land.” The description echoes the covenant promise of the land (Deuteronomy 8:7-10). As a shadow: the physical finding of good pasture types the eschatological rest of Hebrews 4:9 — “there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God” — the ultimate “broad, peaceful land” that the Simeonite migration faintly foreshadows.
41: The Simeonites destroy Hamite settlements in the days of Hezekiah. The Chronicler’s pattern: covenant faithfulness and territorial blessing are connected. Hezekiah’s reign is presented as a messianic anticipation in 2 Chr 29-32 — the most Davidic king since David himself, whose reforming work shadows the ultimate reform Christ brings.
42: Five hundred Simeonites to Mount Seir against the surviving Amalekites. The Amalekites represented persistent, multi-generational opposition to God’s people (Exodus 17:8-16; 1 Sam 15). Their gradual elimination is part of the covenant warfare motif — YHWH’s judgment on those who opposed his people from the beginning, typing Christ’s final defeat of all enemies.
43: “They struck down the remaining Amalekites who had escaped.” The complete elimination of the persistent ancient enemy types Christ’s final defeat of all opponents (1 Corinthians 15:25: “he must reign until he has put all enemies under his feet”; Revelation 19:11-21). The Amalekites who previously “escaped” judgment are finally eliminated — no enemy of God’s people ultimately survives the full sweep of covenant justice.
